p: Team: LS-UDP · Jira board: UDPO · Duration: 12 weeks · Status: Active

ac:rich-text-body.p:
Intern roster:
Benjamin Antinozzi (bpantino@ncsu.edu)
Jack Panning (jackhpanning@gmail.com)

p: Data movement from Azure Snowflake to AWS Snowflake is handled by Snowflake replication, owned by John Thomas. The replicated data lands in a new AWS database with its own structure and naming standards, so source and target are not 1:1. The interns' job is not to move data; it's to give the team confidence the replicated data matches its source under that mapping, and to make the migration's status legible. They will build a migration dashboard for the LS-UDP AWS Migration Project, package the existing Confluence push script as a reusable GitHub Action, build a mapping-driven parity validator, and use that validator to sign off on the replication of one database under UDPO-7851 / UDPO-7866.
All development in this project uses AI tooling as the default delivery approach, with mentor review and sign-off on architecture, quality, and production-readiness decisions.

p.em: Diagram 01 — Intern project flow across the 12-week internship

p: The migration is the team's largest in-flight initiative. Today, cutover readiness is tracked by hand on Confluence pages (Progress Tracker, Open Architecture Questions, weekly WSRs) and parity is verified ad hoc. Both gaps are real, intern-team-sized, and produce tools the team keeps using after the internship ends.

The project also gives both interns a working knowledge of Snowflake, the EDW data model, and the Jira workflow by week 6, which is the prerequisite for being useful on any UDPO ticket. By week 10 they should be able to read a LAB or OBSERVATION bug ticket and understand what's actually broken.

ac:rich-text-body.p: Two interns will execute this project as a shared delivery pod. Suggested split: Intern A leads the Jira-backed dashboard and GitHub Action packaging; Intern B leads the parity validator. Both interns participate in design reviews, demos, and cutover sign-off activities.

h3: Part 1 — Migration dashboard for the LS-UDP AWS Migration Project (weeks 1–4)
p: A web page that auto-refreshes from Jira and renders the current state of the migration. Replaces or augments the hand-maintained Progress Tracker page.

ul.li: Pulls all UDPO issues under epics UDPO-7292, UDPO-7851, UDPO-7866 via the Jira REST API.
Groups by database (VE_PROD, CP_PROD, UDP_PROD_REF_DATA, PF_PROD, etc.) and shows status, owner, open blockers, open architecture questions linked to that database.
Implements board-level filtering so the same dashboard framework can also serve all tickets in the LS-UDP AWS Migration Project board, not only epic-scoped views.

Delivers a production-ready dashboard template package (template HTML, data contract sample, and implementation notes) that can be reused across migration reporting workflows and maintained in the intern repository (mkunath31/Intern-Projects).
Renders as static HTML pushed to Confluence on a schedule via GitHub Actions.

As part of this work, the interns package the team's existing Confluence push script as a reusable GitHub Action. Today the push is run by hand from a project folder; this turns it into a workflow other repos can call.

p: Definition of done: dashboard refreshes on a schedule via GitHub Actions, the push step runs through the reusable action, the team uses the dashboard instead of the hand-maintained tracker for at least one standup, and the interns have documented both the workflow/action runbook and the production-ready dashboard template package.

A Python tool that confirms data in the new AWS database matches its Azure source under the team's source-to-target mapping. The mapping is a config file (YAML or JSON) maintained by the data model owners (Dan Barkman, Brian Burkman, Mike Immrie) and consumed by the validator. The interns do not author the mapping; they consume it.

Database names and any other configurable validator elements (for example environments, table include/exclude lists, and threshold settings) are defined in configuration files rather than hard-coded in the validator.

The validator targets the silver layer first (Manu's domain). If time allows in weeks 11–12, it can be extended to the gold layer (Dharani's domain) as a stretch. Silver is the right starting point because parity matters most where downstream consumers read; gold is derived, so silver-layer parity bugs will propagate upward and need to be caught at silver anyway.
The validator handles three mapping shapes:

ul.li: 1:1 with renames. Source table maps to one target table, columns may be renamed or retyped. Most common case.
1:N (split). Source table maps to multiple target tables. Validator checks each target slice individually and the union.
N:1 (merge). Multiple source tables map to one target table. Validator checks each source contribution against the relevant target subset.

p: For each mapped pair, the validator checks:

ul.li: Mapping coverage: every source column has a defined target (or is explicitly marked as dropped). Every target column has a defined source (or is explicitly marked as derived).
Type compatibility: mapped column types are compatible. Promotions (NUMBER → NUMBER with larger precision, VARCHAR → VARCHAR with larger length) are fine; lossy mappings are flagged.
Row count comparison, scoped by the mapping shape.
Null-rate and distinct-count per mapped column.
Sampled hash check on a stratified row sample using the mapping to align rows.
Replication lag check (max updated_at or equivalent watermark on each side).
HTML report output that lands on Confluence next to the dashboard.

p: Before any code, the interns meet with John Thomas to understand how the replication is configured, how the target database structure is being decided, what guarantees the replication makes, and what failure modes John already monitors. The validator should complement John's monitoring, not duplicate it.

Definition of done: the validator runs against at least three replicated tables covering at least two of the three mapping shapes, John and Manu have reviewed the report format, and the validator is wired into the cutover sign-off checklist for replicated databases. Both interns can run the validator and explain the report output.
h3: Part 3 — Sign off one database's replication (weeks 9–12)
p: Paired with Spandana and coordinating with John Thomas, the interns use the validator from Part 2 to sign off replication parity on one database, observes the cutover, and writes a retro.

ul.li: Pick the database in the design sign-off (Mark + Manu) at the start of week 9. Coordinate with John. Target: low-risk, well-scoped, silver-layer, no open architecture questions.
Run the validator pre-cutover. Resolve any discrepancies with John before sign-off.
Attend the cutover. Re-run the validator post-cutover. Attach the report to the closing ticket.
Write a retro: what the validator caught, what it missed, what to add in v2, and any patterns worth feeding back into John's replication monitoring.
Stretch (weeks 11–12 if cutover slot doesn't materialize, or in parallel if it does): extend the validator to a gold-layer table with Dharani's input.

p: Definition of done: validator reports attached to one database's cutover ticket, retro published on Confluence.

ul.li: The replication itself. John Thomas owns Azure-to-AWS Snowflake replication; the interns verify its output, not the pipeline.
Defining the source-to-target mapping or the new database's structure and naming standards. The interns consume the mapping; they do not author it.
Anything touching PHI in Slack or other channels.
Pipeline rewrites or new ingestion sources (Practice Fusion, Athena, eCW, Office Ally — those are owned by the existing UDPO stories).
Re-architecting the Two-step FTP/Blob load (UDPO-7851 has an open architecture question here; interns shouldn't own that decision).
Power BI re-pointing (UDPO-7848 is its own track).

tr.td: Language
Python 3
Warehouse
Snowflake (Azure and AWS), Snowflake Python connector
Source of truth
Jira REST API, Confluence storage format
Development approach
AI-assisted development by default, with mentor-led review and governance
Hosting / scheduling
GitHub Actions
Output
HTML, pushed via the team's existing Confluence push script

p.em: Diagram 03 — Data flow and where the interns' deliverables plug in

tr.td: Onboarding, access (Jira, Snowflake dev, Confluence, GitHub). Read the Progress Tracker, Open Architecture Questions, and the V2 epic UDPO-7292.
End-of-week 1:1, confirm access
Jira API spike. Pull all UDPO issues under the migration epics into a local script. Sketch the dashboard data model.
Review the data model with mentor
Dashboard v1 rendering locally. Reviewed by mentor.
Demo to mentor

Dashboard scheduled and pushed to Confluence. Documented in runbook. Meet with John Thomas to learn the replication setup. Meet with Dan, Brian, and Mike to learn the mapping and naming standards. Validator design sign-off by Mark Kunath and Manu before week 5 work starts.
Demo dashboard to team in standup. Spandana joins the John and data-model meetings.
Consume the source-to-target mapping. Validator spike on a silver-layer 1:1 mapped pair with renames, checking row count and schema mapping coverage.
Review approach with Spandana and John. Pull in mentors (Mark Kunath and Manu) as needed.
Validator v1 on silver: 1:1 case complete — coverage, type compatibility, row count, null rate, distinct count, replication lag check. Tested against one 1:1 mapped silver table.
Code review with Spandana. Reach out to Dean if anything is structurally unclear.
Add sampled hash check. Extend to handle 1:N (split) and N:1 (merge) mapping shapes. Run against three replicated silver tables covering at least two shapes.
Review reports with John; include Manu as needed
Validator wired into the cutover sign-off checklist. Documented, including how to add a new mapping to the config.
Demo to team
Target DB pick signed off by Mark Kunath and Manu, coordinating with John. Plan the sign-off.
Pick reviewed before any prod access
Pre-cutover: run the validator against the target database, work through any discrepancies with John.
Pre-cutover sign-off review
Attend cutover. Re-run validator post-cutover. Close the ticket.
Cutover observed
Retro, internship write-up, knowledge transfer to whoever inherits the dashboard and validator.
Final demo to broader team

ul.li: Dashboard is the team's primary reference for migration status by week 5.
Validator is part of the replication sign-off checklist by week 8.
Validator has been used to sign off replication parity on one database by week 12.
Both interns can read and triage a UDPO bug on LAB / OBSERVATION / CLINICAL_PROCEDURE without help by week 10.

h2: Risks and how we'll manage them

tr.td: Access delays (Snowflake, Jira API, Confluence write, push-script repo) eat week 1.
Mark Kunath files access tickets the week before start and confirms all access on day 1.
Security training competes with project time, especially in weeks 1–2.
Plan for it. Week 1 milestones are deliberately light (onboarding + reading) to absorb training load.
The source-to-target mapping isn't ready by week 5.

Hard dependency. Mark Kunath checks in on mapping status in weeks 3 and 4. If it slips, the interns work on the dashboard's polish, the GitHub Action, or the documentation runbook instead of starting on the validator blind. Don't let them build against a guess.
Mapping is unstable through weeks 5–8 (definitions keep changing).

Expected, and the validator design absorbs this: mapping is a config file, not code. The interns work against whatever version is current and refresh the report when the config changes. They do not chase mapping changes inside the validator's own logic.
A target structure shows up that doesn't fit 1:1, 1:N, or N:1 (e.g. M:N joins, derived/calculated columns).

The validator flags these as "out of validator scope" in the report rather than silently failing. The intern documents what's not covered. Closing those gaps becomes a v2 ticket, not an intern problem.
The validator overlaps with monitoring John already has, and feels like an audit.
The week-4 meeting with John is for alignment, not announcement. Intern shows up to learn what John already checks and designs the validator to fill the gap, not duplicate the work.
Intern picks a database for Part 3 that has hidden complexity (open architecture question, unusual upstream).
Mark Kunath, Manu, and John pick the candidate together in week 9. Anything tied to an open architecture question on the tracker is off-limits.
Validator surfaces real production discrepancies on replicated tables.
Good — that's the point. Escalate to John and the database owner, log as a separate ticket. Don't let it derail the interns' timeline.
Part 3 sign-off slips past week 12 because the replication cutover slips.
Acceptable. Parts 1 and 2 are independently valuable; the internship is a success at the end of week 8.

ul.li: Manager: weekly 1:1 with each intern. Owns career conversations, scope decisions, and demo sign-off.
Day-to-day support: Spandana. First point of contact for questions, unblocks, and code review.
Mentors: Mark Kunath and Manu Verma. They guide project direction and major sign-off decisions for both interns.
Validator design and target-database pick: owned jointly by Mark Kunath and Manu Verma. Design sign-off at end of week 4, before validator work starts. DB pick at start of week 9.

Senior developer / code review: Dean. Ad-hoc resource for technical questions and deeper code review. Most experienced developer on the team. Intern reaches out when stuck or when Spandana wants a second opinion.

Silver layer owner: Manu Verma. Mentor and stakeholder for the validator's silver-layer checks (weeks 5–10). Reviews findings and helps the interns make sense of silver-layer discrepancies. Touchpoint as needed.
Gold layer owner: Dharani. Stakeholder if and when the validator extends to gold (stretch goal in weeks 11–12).
Replication owner: John Thomas. Stakeholder for the validator — the interns work with him from week 4 onward to understand the replication and align on what the validator should check.

Data model owners: Dan Barkman, Brian Burkman, Mike Immrie. Own the source-to-target mapping and the new database's structure and naming standards. The intern consumes their mapping; brief touchpoint in week 4, and again when shape questions come up.
Infrastructure (AWS, Azure, GitHub / GitHub Actions): Sreeni and Kevin. Ad-hoc resource for infrastructure questions including Snowflake account topology, networking, IAM, and the Actions setup.
Standup: both interns join from week 2.
Demo cadence: end of weeks 4, 8, 12 to the broader team.

p.em: Diagram 02 — Who the interns talk to for what

p: The interns take Veradigm's security learning sessions in parallel with the project work. This is not optional or scheduled around the project — block time for it on the calendar from week 1.

ul.li: Expect 2–4 hours per week on training, more in the first two weeks.
HIPAA / PHI handling must be completed before any access to production Snowflake (week 9 at the latest, ideally by week 6).
Spandana confirms training status before granting prod access for Part 3.
Where will the source-to-target mapping live (which repo, which format) and what's the cadence for updates? Dan, Brian, and Mike own the content; we need a path for the validator to read it.
